evidence_excerpt: At 2, 7, 9, and 18 months after ileal tumor resection, there was remission of Cushing’s syndrome and diabetes, improved fitness and mood, with residual obesity and hypercholesterolemia; antihypertensive therapy was reduced (two of four medications discontinued).

quality_of_life.evidence_excerpt: Long-term follow-up showed remission of hypercortisolism and diabetes; however, he still suffered from obesity and hypercholesterolemia.

timeline_events.evidence_excerpt: At 2 months post-surgery, dexamethasone suppression was normal, with remission of Cushing’s syndrome and no evidence of diabetes.
Ga-68 DOTATOC PET at 5 months post-surgery showed no pathologic uptake.
By 18 months, physical fitness and mood had improved considerably, and two of four antihypertensive medications were discontinued.
At 18 months post-surgery, continued remission of Cushing’s syndrome and no diabetes were documented; bone densitometry was normal.

notes_on_uncertainty: The definitive diagnosis/localization of the primary ileal ACTH-secreting tumor was established in December 2007 prior to resection and used as the reference. Follow-up is reported up to 18 months; outcomes beyond that are not available. Functional status is inferred from narrative improvement and persistent obesity with partial reduction of antihypertensive therapy.
